Precipitation during the months of June through August was 8 inches or less over a good portion of Litchfield County, which is 50 to 75 percent of normal. The precipitation deficit, along with a hot summer (3 to 5 degrees above normal for a good portion of the county), led to drought conditions developing. Severe drought conditions crept into the far eastern portion of the county by 18 August, continuing through the end of the month and into September. Streamflows and reservoir levels were below normal, with the Salisbury Reservoir reporting levels less than 80% of normal by the middle of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry precipitation impacted northwestern Connecticut on the 6th and 7th. Periods of freezing rain occurred from the afternoon of the 6th into the morning of the 7th, mainly over the higher terrain. Reports of up to a quarter inch of ice accretion were received. The freezing rain quickly turned to a brief period of snow during the afternoon of the 7th as a strong low pressure system passed south of the area, with one to two inches of accumulation. In addition, westerly winds became strong during the afternoon and evening of the 7th as the low continued to strengthen.
A mid-level cold pool provided the instability for scattered severe thunderstorms, with large hail and damaging winds in northern Connecticut and western Massachusetts during the mid-afternoon hours.

A robust backdoor cold front for early July standards in southern New England provided the forcing for some strong thunderstorms in northern Connecticut and western Massachusetts in the afternoon.  With high humidity and precipitable water values (near two inches), flash flooding occurred when two inches of rain fell in less than an hour in the Hartford metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 2 inches of rain fell in less than 1 hour.  In Hartford, numerous cars were stuck in flash flood waters... on Franklin Street at South Street; Buckingham, John, Hamilton, Bonner, and Brown Streets, and Hillside Avenue.  A small portion of Airport Road in Hartford, east of Wethersfield Avenue, buckled inward as a result of the heavy rainfall.  A family on Sisson Avenue was forced from their home when water entered the home. A basement was flooded on Wolcott Street. Reports were from a combination of law enforcement, amateur radio, and newspaper and tv stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado was spawned and briefly touched down in Westport as Tropical Storm Isaias impacted Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF1 Tornado confirmed, severe damage to house on Surf Road with Roof ripped off and several pine trees snapped off.||A discrete, low topped storm cell, moving north northwest from |Long Island Sound and over Saugatuck Shores in Westport CT, |produced a waterspout, that made landfall as an EF1 tornado |around 140pm on August 4 2020. ||The waterspout was captured on video by a private meteorologist |as it was moving towards Saugatuck Shores in Westport CT around |140pm. The tornado produced severe damage to a house on Surf Rd, |with the roof being ripped off, as well as portions of the second |floor's supporting wall structure. This debris was tossed about |30-50 feet north on the property. In addition, the tops of several|pine trees in the front and side yard were either sheared or |snapped off. The tornado likely quickly lifted and possibly |tracked north as a funnel cloud for another 1 to 2 miles, before |dissipating. The funnel cloud could have touched down as a |waterspout on the Saugatuck River, south of Route 1, based on an |eyewitness report from S Compo Rd in Westport, CT. Otherwise, |downstream damage reports are inconclusive for tornado damage, but|consistent with the damaging 40 to 50 mph straight line sustained|winds and 60 to 70 mph gusts observed between 2pm and 5pm across |southwestern CT from Tropical Storm Isaias.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region. Heat indices reached the 95 to 100 degree range during the afternoons of Monday, August 10th and Tuesday, August 11th.
A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region. Heat indices reached the 95 to 100 degree range during the afternoons of Monday, August 10th and Tuesday, August 11th.

A strong low pressure system to the west ushered in unseasonably warm and moist air to northwestern Connecticut on the 15th. A line of thunderstorms formed along an approaching cold front during the late evening hours, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A couple of short swaths of intense damage occurred resulting in numerous downed trees, likely from microbursts, around Roxbury, Harwinton, and Litchfield. Lingering power outages resulted in the closure of Litchfield schools the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure impacted the region Christmas Eve into Christmas Day. Moderate to heavy rain developed out ahead of the approaching cold front in an anomalously moist environment, leading to river flooding along the Yantic River. Precipitable water values on the 12Z 12/25 sounding from Upton, NY were around 1.5 inches, a record for the date based on the Storm Prediction Center's sounding climatology. This resulted in widespread rainfall totals of 1.5-2 inches across southeastern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yantic River at Yantic, CT rose above its minor flood stage of 9.0 feet at 12:00pm EST. The river then continued to rise, reaching moderate flood stage (9.5 feet) at 6:45pm EST before cresting at 9.58 feet at 7:45pm EST. The river fell back below moderate flood stage at 8:15pm EST and below minor flood stage at 11:30pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with high winds ahead of it passed through during the late evening of December 24, 2020 into early morning hours of December 25, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wind gusts of 58-71 mph recorded, including a measured wind gust of 71 mph at 1131 PM December 24, 2020 local standard time in Greenwich. Additionally, a trained spotter reported that a 60 foot oak tree was uprooted and leaning on power lines 6 miles west of New Canaan at 249 AM local standard time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along the Middle Atlantic coast on Wednesday December 16, 2020. The low deepened as it tracked northward towards Long Island, NY through the night on December 16, 2020. Arctic high pressure situated over southeast Canada provided a cold and dry air mass ahead of the low supporting moderate to locally heavy snowfall at the onset. The low pressure ultimately tracked from near Atlantic City, NJ to about 50 miles south of Fire Island, NY before sliding offshore Thursday December 17, 2020. This track led to a brief period of a wintry mix of snow and sleet across southwest Connecticut and a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and rain across southeast Connecticut during the earlier morning hours on December 17, 2020.  Once the low began moving away from Long Island, the wintry mix changed back over to snow and any location that rose above freezing fell significantly below freezing. The storm significantly impacted travel during the evening commute on Wednesday December 16, 2020 and the morning commute on Thursday December 17, 2020. Many accidents resulted from the snow and icy conditions. Snowfall rates ranged from 1 to 2 inches per hour Wednesday evening. Total snowfall ranged from around 6 to 12 inches with locally higher amounts up to 14.5 inches across the northern tier of Fairfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported 12 inches of snow was reported in both Wallingford and Guilford, CT. CoCoRaHS, amateur radio, and trained spotters measured 7 to 12 inches of snow. The Meriden Airport ASOS measured a 39 mph wind gust at 10:53 am on December 17, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation during the months of June through August was 8 inches or less over a good portion of Litchfield County, which is 50 to 75 percent of normal. The precipitation deficit, along with a hot summer (3 to 5 degrees above normal for a good portion of the county), led to drought conditions developing. The dry weather continued into September, with much of the county seeing 50 to 75 percent of normal precipitation during the month. This allowed severe drought conditions, which had developed by mid-August, to continue for the eastern third of the county. The far eastern portion of the county reached extreme drought conditions by 29 September. Streamflows and reservoir levels were below normal, with the Salisbury Reservoir reporting levels 44% of normal by the 27 September. The US Department of Agriculture declared Litchfield County a primary natural disaster area as a result of the drought.

A strong warm front moving northeast across southern New England and an approaching short wave trough combined to produce three tornadoes in western Connecticut during the early evening hours of Sunday, August 2nd, 2020. The first tornado touched down near the town of Sharon, where there was damage to several greenhouses and multiple downed trees. The storm then tracked towards Falls Village where numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. The storm continued towards the town of Norfolk where most of the damage was done to trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service storm survey team confirms an EF0 tornado in Sharon, CT. The tornado struck a nursery along the New York/Connecticut border. Large trees were uprooted. The metal infrastructure of greenhouses was destroyed. Eyewitnesses reported|garden supplies rotating in the air above the nursery. Down the road from the nursery, additional uprooted trees were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of supercell thunderstorms tracked through Litchfield County along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. These storms resulted in numerous damage reports, especially in the area from Kent to Woodbury. Eighty percent of the town of Roxbury was without power as a result of the storms. In addition, two brief EF0 tornadoes occurred in Kent and Woodbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires on Blackville Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in close proximity to a departing warm front triggered numerous severe thunderstorms across Northern New Jersey. A tornado touched down in Bethany to North Haven in New Haven County. Also, multiple microbursts affected parts of New Haven County which included, East Haven, North Haven, the Branfords, and Guilford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF1 Tornado staring in Bethany, CT and ending in North Haven, CT. Maximum path width of 500 yards and a path length of 11.1 miles.||Based on a National Weather Service damage survey done in |conjunction with CT Division of Emergency Management and Homeland |Security and local CT town emergency managements, it has been |determined that a strong EF1 tornado, with maximum wind speed of |110 mph, tracked southeast from Bethany to North Haven CT. ||The tornado first touched down in a forested area to the |southeast of Judd Hill Rd in Bethany CT. The tornado tracked |southeast over primarily forested areas from Amity Rd, to Munson |Rd towards Litchfield Tpke, creating a path of damage about 75 |yards wide, with hardwood tree damage consistent with wind speeds |of 80 to 90 mph. ||The path of damage widened to around 300 yards as the tornado |tracked southeast towards Lake Bethany. Structural damage, |including significant roof damage to several homes, and snapped |hardwood trees indicated wind speeds of around 100 mph in this |area.||The tornado path continued southeast for another 4 miles to near |the town center of Hamden CT, with tree and structural damage |indicative of wind speeds of 70 to 80 mph. The intensity picked up|significantly as the tornado approached the center of Hamden, as |evidenced by extensive damage to numerous buildings, including the|flat roof of a 2 story building across from Hamden Town Hall |being torn apart. Wind speeds are estimated to be around 100 mph |based on the damage to these buildings, bent metal fencing around|town hall, and uprooted and snapped trees.||The tornado reached maximum strength and width from this point on|as it continued southeast across Wilbur Cross Parkway, Interstate|91, and down to the intersection of Arrowdale and Thompson St in |North Haven, CT. Tremendous hardwood tree damage and structural |damage was indicative of wind speeds of 110 mph and an expanded |width of 500 yards. It is a this point that the tornado appears to|have dissipated with its destructive straight line winds fanning |out to the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front moving northeast across southern New England and an approaching short wave trough combined to produce three tornadoes in western Connecticut during the early evening hours of Sunday, August 2nd, 2020. The first tornado touched down near the town of Sharon, where there was damage to several greenhouses and multiple downed trees. The storm then tracked towards Falls Village where numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. The storm continued towards the town of Norfolk where most of the damage was done to trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service storm survey team confirms an EF1 tornado in Falls Village, CT. The survey team observed many snapped and uprooted trees along Lime Rock Station Road near River Road. Additional downed trees were noted along the path of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias tracked northeast from the eastern Carolinas across the mid-Hudson Valley and into New England. The center of the storm passed close to Albany, NY on August 4th. This storm brought tropical storm force winds and moderate rainfall to western Connecticut through the period. These winds caused widespread damage with numerous reports of downed trees and wires across Connecticut. Power outages were also widespread with over 60,000 outages across Litchfield county and over 700,000 state-wide. On August 5th, 24 hours after the storm exited the area, 67% of Litchfield County was still without power. Three days later, on August 8th, 40% of the county was still without power. An Emergency Disaster Declaration was approved by President Trump on August 7th, 2020. Connecticut Division of Emergency Management and Homeland Security claimed that it would take just over 2 million dollars to clean up the damage across Litchfield county alone, and about 35 million for clean up across the state.

A pair of supercell thunderstorms tracked through Litchfield County along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. These storms resulted in numerous damage reports, especially in the area from Kent to Woodbury. Eighty percent of the town of Roxbury was without power as a result of the storms. In addition, two brief EF0 tornadoes occurred in Kent and Woodbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage photos supplied by the public suggest that a brief tornado occurred on Quanopaug Trail in Woodbury.  Numerous trees were downed in various directions in a wooded area. Maximum winds were estimated to be around 85 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front moving northeast across southern New England and an approaching short wave trough combined to produce three tornadoes in western Connecticut during the early evening hours of Sunday, August 2nd, 2020. The first tornado touched down near the town of Sharon, where there was damage to several greenhouses and multiple downed trees. The storm then tracked towards Falls Village where numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. The storm continued towards the town of Norfolk where most of the damage was done to trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Albany received photo and eyewitness accounts consistent with an EF0 tornado near Norfolk, CT. Therefore, an EF0 tornado was confirmed and was on the ground for approximately 0.5 miles in length, with a path width of approximately 100 yards. This tornado occurred with the same supercell that produced tornadoes in Sharon, CT, Falls Village, CT, and Sandisfield, MA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced heavy snow, strong winds, and minor coastal flooding in southern New England.  This was a tough storm to forecast.  Although it was not deep low pressure and although it passed just south of the 40N/70W benchmark, its precipitation shield was expansive because of a broad 700 mb low over southeast New England, abundant moisture, and a very cold air mass in place.  By far, the heaviest snow fell to the north (Laconia, NH) and west (Binghamton, NY), where more than 3 feet fell.  But amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches across much of southern New England.  There was some minor coastal flooding at the time of high tide and also a flash freeze in eastern Massachusetts as the storm pulled away to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 9 to 16 inches. Some representative reports included 16.0 inches in Suffield, 14.5 inches in Burlingotn, 13.8 inches in West Hartford, 12.3 inches at Bradley International Airport in Windsor Locks (KBDL), 12.0 inches in Wethersfield, and 11.0 inches in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of supercell thunderstorms tracked through Litchfield County along a warm front during the afternoon of the 27th. These storms resulted in numerous damage reports, especially in the area from Kent to Woodbury. Eighty percent of the town of Roxbury was without power as a result of the storms. In addition, two brief EF0 tornadoes occurred in Kent and Woodbury.

An anomalously deep, full-latitude mid-level trough over the Mississippi Valley caused a strong frontal system to move up the Appalachians. It brought strong to damaging winds, heavy rain with minor flooding, and well above normal temperatures to southern New England early on Christmas Day.  Winds generally gusted to 40 to 60 mph, except 65 to 70 mph along the southeast Massachusetts coast.  South winds were blowing more than 100 mph only 2000 feet above the ground in eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, but despite temperatures in the lower 60s there, a surface inversion was strong enough to prevent these very damaging winds from reaching the surface.  Two to four inches of rain fell across the region, with the highest totals from central Rhode Island northwestward across northern Connecticut and portions of western and central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occurred in Hartford County, with a CWOP station in Simsbury recording 4.01 inches.  In Bristol, a CWOP station recorded 3.66 inches.  Other readings ranged from 2.00 inches in Wethersfield to 3.48 inches in Glastonbury.  At 551 AM EST, the ASOS at the Bradley International Airport (KBDL) in Windsor Locks measured a wind gust to 62 mph.  At 453 PM EST, the ASOS at the Hartford-Brainard Airport (KHFD) measured a gust to 48 mph.  In Burlington at 441 AM EST, a tree and wires were down on Scoville Road and at 854 AM EST, a tree and wires were down near Cambridge Street.  In Plainville at 815 AM EST, a tree was down on Washington Street.  In Canton at 1005 AM EST, a tree was down on Washington Street near Mass Audubon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying storm system produced heavy rain (1-2.5 inches) which changed to a period of heavy snow in the higher elevations of northern CT. The low pressure system crossed between Nantucket and Cape Cod before lifting northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a wide variation of snowfall totals across Tolland County. In the higher elevations of northeast Tolland County there was a public report of 10 inches in Union; and several trained spotter, CO-OP observer, and CoCoRaHS reports of 5.5 to 6.0 inches in Tolland and Stafford.

Isaias' intensity weakened back to Tropical Storm strength as its forward speed increased to near 30 mph during the early morning of August 4th.||The center of Isaias passed about 65 miles west of New York City at 3 pm on August 4, 2020. Tropical storm force winds extended well east of the center of circulation. The highest sustained wind speeds ranged from 35 to 55 mph, with gusts 60 to 70 mph. ||Isaias brought sustained tropical storm winds with gusts to near hurricane force across Southern Connecticut, resulting in widespread wind damage and power outages. There was also a confirmed EF-1 tornado in Westport, CT around 1:40 pm. Coastal impacts were minimal, and mainly from high surf along the coast. Rainfall amounts of 1/2 to 1 1/2 inches were observed across southwestern portions CT. ||One death resulted from high winds:|In Naugatuck, New Haven, one man died after being struck by a falling tree while trying to move branches in front of his car on Andrew Mountain Road.||Preliminary damage cost estimates for Public Assistance are:||New Haven   $ 9,610,561  +  $ 415,000   =  $ 10,025,561||Fairfield        $ 7,364,714  +  $1,625,000  = $ 8,989,714||New London $ 1,259,191  +  $220,000     = $ 1,479,191||Middlesex    $ 1,274,266   + $45,000        = $ 1,319,266||The number of power outages, statewide peaked around 752,225 at 8:00 AM on August 5th. The state lost around 1 percent of their tree population.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed throughout the county. This resulted in numerous power outages. ||Tweed New Haven Airport reported a gust of 56 mph at 253 pm LST. Lighthouse Point reported a maximum sustained wind of 48 mph at 218 pm LST and a peak gust of 66 mph at 228 pm LST. ||There were no reports of coastal flooding as the highest storm surge did not coincide with high tide and was during a period of low astronomical tides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in close proximity to a departing warm front triggered numerous severe thunderstorms across Northern New Jersey. A tornado touched down in Bethany to North Haven in New Haven County. Also, multiple microbursts affected parts of New Haven County which included, East Haven, North Haven, the Branfords, and Guilford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down on Danbury Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep, full-latitude mid-level trough over the Mississippi Valley caused a strong frontal system to move up the Appalachians. It brought strong to damaging winds, heavy rain with minor flooding, and well above normal temperatures to southern New England early on Christmas Day.  Winds generally gusted to 40 to 60 mph, except 65 to 70 mph along the southeast Massachusetts coast.  South winds were blowing more than 100 mph only 2000 feet above the ground in eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, but despite temperatures in the lower 60s there, a surface inversion was strong enough to prevent these very damaging winds from reaching the surface.  Two to four inches of rain fell across the region, with the highest totals from central Rhode Island northwestward across northern Connecticut and portions of western and central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally were gusting to 50 to 55 mph.  In Woodstock at 750 AM EST, a tree was down at the intersection of Routes 197 and 198.  In Brooklyn at 810 AM EST, a tree and wires and part of a utility pole were snapped on Providence Road at Prince Hill Road.  In Pomfret at 838 AM EST, a tree was down on Angel Road at Route 244.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass filtered into the region and interacted with moisture from Tropical Cyclone Zeta. This resulted in a light, early season snowfall event with highest amounts of 3 to 5 inches mainly across the Litchfield Hills in western Connecticut.

Lingering drought conditions from an abnormally dry summer continued into the month of October for eastern portions of Litchfield county. At the middle of the month, the eastern third of Litchfield county was in D2 to D3 drought conditions. But thanks to near normal precipitation throughout the  the month, these conditions improved to now only include the far eastern tip of the county in D2 status. The US Department of Agriculture declared Litchfield County a primary natural disaster area as a result of the drought.

A squall line formed in New York State then raced eastward in the late afternoon and early evening, gathering strength as it raced across Massachusetts, northeast Connecticut, and northern Rhode Island. Hundreds of thousands of people were left without power in southern New England, as there was widespread tree and power line damage from winds generally gusting to between 50 and 80 mph. The Storm Prediction Center officially classified it as a derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Enfield, multiple trees were downed at several locations in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high-end severe weather event unfolded across the Northeast on Wednesday October 7, 2020. A line of thunderstorms originated across New York state and moved eastward into New England during the afternoon hours, producing widespread damage. This line brushed northern portions of Connecticut, producing some tree damage across northern Litchfield County. This event was classified as a serial derecho based on the 320 mile long damage swath and distribution of significant wind gusts (75 mph and above).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved from coastal Virginia eastern New York state, near the Connecticut/Massachusetts border on Tuesday, August 4.  As the storm reached the New York City area, southerly winds were maximizing across southern New England, causing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 228 PM EDT and approximately 530 PM EDT, numerous trees and wires were downed.  Reports of downed trees and wires were received from the towns of Tolland, Andover, Willington, Vernon, Columbia, and Hebron.  In Hebron, a tree and wires were down on a car, but there were no injuries. Municipal damage claims were approximately $850,000 and uninsured home/small business claims were near $420,000. That does not count individual homeowners' insurance deductibles, etc.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northward off the East Coast toward New York City on Wednesday, December 16, then eastward to just off Cape Cod on Thursday, December 17. As a result, cold enough air north of this low led to moderate to heavy snowfall across all of eastern New York and western New England.  Snow started across northwestern Connecticut during the late afternoon hours on Wednesday, December 16 and continued through the overnight hours before gradually winding down during the morning hours on Thursday, December 17.||Bands of heavy snow occurred with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour reported at times. Snowfall totals generally ranged between 10 and 20 inches across northwestern Connecticut.||Schools across Litchfield County were closed on Thursday, December 17. The governor banned tractor trailers from limited access highways during the storm. The city hall building in Torrington was closed due to the snow.||Key impacts: traffic accidents, transportation delays, school closures, government closures.

An area of low pressure tracking from the Great Lakes to Hudson Bay advected in an unseasonably warm air mass into the region from Thursday, December 24 to Friday, December 25, 2020. Rain gradually overspread the region from west to east during the day on December 24 with the steadiest, heaviest rainfall during the overnight hours and early morning hours of December 25. Rain showers continued through the day on December 25 and changed to snow showers during the evening and overnight hours of December 25-26 as colder air returned.||The region still dealt with nearly the entire snowpack from the blockbuster winter storm from December 16-17. While the snow compacted over time, very little water was lost from the snow. Observations concluded that between 1.50 to 2.50 inches of water was in the snowpack prior to this event and most if not all of this snow melted. However, impactful flooding was not noted during this event.||Rainfall totals across northwestern Connecticut were between 1.00 and 3.00 inches. These amounts do not include the additional 1.50 to 2.50 inches of water that melted from the snowpack. In addition to the rainfall, gusty winds accompanied the event with wind gusts reaching 45 to 60 mph, resulting in numerous reports of downed trees across the region.||Key Impacts: tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed across Litchfield County at numerous locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved from coastal Virginia eastern New York state, near the Connecticut/Massachusetts border on Tuesday, August 4.  As the storm reached the New York City area, southerly winds were maximizing across southern New England, causing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 238 PM EDT and 630 PM EDT, there was widespread damage from fallen trees and power lines.  At 351 PM EDT, a wind gust to 56 mph was recorded at the Bradley International Airport (KBDL) in Windsor Locks.  At 455 PM EDT, a wind gust to 52 mph (45 kt) was measured at Hartford-Brainard Airport (KHFD).  In West Hartford, trees and power lines were reported down in West Hartford, Newington, Enfield, and Burlington.  Utility poles were downed in South Windsor.  Trees were reported to have fallen onto houses in New Britain, Marlborough, Wethersfield (house crushed on Olney Street), East Windsor (heavy damage to a home on Pleasant Street),  Bristol, and Rocky Hill.  A tree was down onto a condo complex in South Windsor.  In Wethersfield, the roof of an apartment complex was lifted off (on Maple Street).  In New Britain, part of the roof was blown off a building in the New Brite Plaza.  In Burlington, wires fell onto an occupied car, but there were no injuries.  In a separate event, a tree fell onto a vehicle on Route 4 in Burlington, but the woman inside was not injured.  There were widespread power outages. Municipal damage claims were approximately $6.9 million and uninsured home/small business claims were near $2.0 million.  That does not count individual homeowners' insurance deductibles, etc.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved from coastal Virginia eastern New York state, near the Connecticut/Massachusetts border on Tuesday, August 4.  As the storm reached the New York City area, southerly winds were maximizing across southern New England, causing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From mainly 3 PM EDT to 6 PM EDT, scattered tree damage occurred in Windham County. The majority of damage went unreported.  However, we did receive a report of a tree down blocking Route 198 in Chaplin at 6 PM EDT.  At 452 PM EDT, the ASOS at the Windham Airport (KIJD) recorded a wind gust to 53 mph (46 kt). Municipal damage claims were approximately $532,000 and uninsured home/small business claims were near $30,000. That does not count individual homeowners' insurance deductibles, etc.
